description: Bug-hunt benchmark manifest v1. Contains planted bugs across 4 difficulty tiers and false-positive traps. Ground truth for scoring.

bugs.description: SQL injection in handle_list_users — the search parameter is interpolated directly into the query string via f-string, allowing arbitrary SQL execution.
MD5 used for password hashing — MD5 is cryptographically broken for password storage. Should use bcrypt/scrypt/argon2.
Plaintext password logged — logger.info includes the raw password in the log message.
Hardcoded admin token in source code — ADMIN_TOKEN is a secret committed to the codebase.
Path traversal in handle_file_download — filename from URL is joined directly to 'uploads' dir with no sanitization. An attacker can use '../' to read arbitrary files.
Admin export endpoint has no authentication check — /api/admin/export returns all user data including password hashes to anyone.

Broken authentication — authenticate() uses the raw token as a user ID for database lookup. Tokens returned by login are just user IDs (integers), making session impersonation trivial by guessing sequential IDs.
Race condition in PipelineStats — increment_ingested and increment_failed mutate shared state without acquiring self.lock. The lock field exists but is never used.
Off-by-one in DataStore.query — uses [start:end] where end = start + page_size - 1, which returns page_size - 1 items instead of page_size. Should be start + page_size.
Silent exception swallowing in process_file — bare 'except Exception: pass' hides all errors including data corruption, permission issues, and encoding failures. Files may silently fail to process.
Archive race condition — the check-then-rename pattern (if not dest.exists → rename) is vulnerable to TOCTOU race conditions. Two concurrent pipeline instances could collide.

Session token truncated to 8 chars for storage key — SessionStore uses token[:8] as the dict key. A 64-char token is reduced to 8 chars of entropy from a 36-char alphabet (≈41 bits). This enables collision attacks and brute-force session hijacking.

Predictable session tokens — generate_session_token seeds Python's random module with deterministic material (time + user_id + pid). An attacker who knows the approximate login time can reconstruct the token. Should use secrets.token_hex().
Non-constant-time password comparison — verify_password uses == instead of hmac.compare_digest, enabling timing attacks to recover password hashes character by character.
Mutable default argument — process_request uses context: dict = {} which shares a single dict across all calls, causing cross-request cache pollution. Classic Python gotcha.

normalize_username allows Unicode confusable attacks — only checks isalnum() which passes Unicode letters. An attacker could register a visually identical username using Cyrillic or other lookalike characters. Should restrict to ASCII or apply NFKC normalization.

traps.description: getattr on user input — LOOKS dangerous but the whitelist check on line 28 (frozenset membership test) ensures only safe str methods are called. This is a false positive trap.
process_order appears to have an unbound variable 'result' — but all branches of the if/elif/else chain assign result before use. Complete coverage. Not a bug.
time.sleep looks like a timing oracle — but it's actually constant-time padding (pads UP to 500ms) which is a correct timing-attack mitigation. Not a vulnerability.

Hardcoded default signing key — LOOKS dangerous but the code raises RuntimeError in production (line 146), only falls back to test key in dev/CI. There's also an env var override. This is intentional defense-in-depth.

Broad except clause — LOOKS like exception swallowing but the error is logged with full traceback (exc_info=True) and re-raised as a specific RuntimeError with 'from e' chain. This is a legitimate error-handling pattern for external service wrappers.
